weather: 12–14°C, fog, mostly_clear, overcast, rain, wind 10–17 km/h
narrative:
This morning at dawn in Tilden Regional Park, a chorus of American Crow calls filled the air with their distinctive "caw-caw" sounds, punctuated by the melodic trills of California Towhee. As the sun rose higher, the park buzzed with activity, particularly as the flocks of California Towhee were most active during this time. By late morning and early afternoon, the American Crow's presence was more noticeable, their constant murmurs mingling with those of the towhees in a symphony of bird song that echoed through the hills.

The American Crow is a new entry in this monitoring window. The quiet from Dark-eyed Junco, Lesser Goldfinch, and Oak Titmouse is not surprising: rain and wind push the acoustic noise floor high enough to bury softer songs, while the juncos have almost certainly departed for higher-elevation breeding grounds — their late-March peak here marked the end of winter residency, not a population. Towhee breeding season just opened — dawn calls are territorial boundary checks. Junco April absence = normal spring departure to Sierra, not rain. Watch for Orange-crowned Warbler on clearing mornings.
